questions.text: I often notice that a person's feet or hands tell a different story than their words.
I often help resolve group disagreements by finding common ground.
I believe that I should unapologetically pursue the things that excite me.
I rarely overrule a group decision, even when I think there is a better option.
I feel that my emotional intensity sometimes makes it hard to think clearly.
I find it hard to tell when someone is being sarcastic versus sincere.
I hold the view that suffering places a moral obligation on every person who becomes aware of it, and that obligation is not optional.
I treat every commitment I make as a binding agreement with myself.
I rarely make backup plans because I trust my ability to improvise if things go sideways.
I believe that the ability to process accounts of human tragedy without personal emotional impact is an asset in any decision-making role.
I am the kind of person others describe as unflappable.
I believe that the ability to evaluate need without emotional bias is what makes genuinely fair decision-making possible.
I believe my skills and abilities exist primarily to serve the people around me.
I believe that I should be my own first priority in life.
I focus on the literal meaning of words rather than the context surrounding them.
I believe that my emotional equilibrium is something I was born with rather than something I cultivated.
I'd rather try something immediately and learn from the result than spend time planning it out.
I would rather lead a small group than follow in a large one.
